VERIFICATION · ANALYSIS

The credibility
gap.

Why self-reported emissions data is the next subprime — and what the climate finance system must fix before the data fails.

D
DEFINE Research
Editorial team · Bengaluru & Boston · January 2026 · 9 minute read
KEY FINDINGS

What you need to know

  1. Self-reported emissions data diverges materially from independent measurement. Satellite observations have found US oil and gas methane emissions to be four times higher than EPA estimates; US cities under-report their own greenhouse gas inventories by 18.3% on average; corporate Scope 3 emissions more than double when properly harmonized.
  2. ESG ratings are structurally unreliable. MIT Sloan finds cross-rater correlation on ESG at 0.61. Mainstream credit ratings correlate at 0.99. Climate finance is being priced off an information layer an order of magnitude noisier than the credit system.
  3. $5 trillion of sustainable finance is stacked on this unverified primitive. The global sustainable bond market is now the largest dedicated source of climate-aligned market finance. Voluntary carbon markets, sustainability-linked loans, and transition finance all depend on the same data foundation.
  4. Enforcement is escalating. The first criminal fraud case in voluntary carbon markets was filed in October 2024. Greenwashing fines now total tens of millions. The ECB found that 60% of European banks lack an integrated climate stress-testing framework, citing data gaps as the central impediment.
  5. The fix is not more disclosure. The credit market solved its data problem with credit bureaus, FICO scores, and verification infrastructure — not by asking borrowers to fill out longer forms. Climate finance needs the architectural equivalent: asset-level, independent, federated verification.

In September 2015, Mark Carney stood at Lloyd's of London and warned the insurance industry of a climate Minsky moment — a sudden financial-stability shock from climate risks that markets had failed to price.[1] His argument was structural, not predictive: that the catastrophic costs of climate change would fall outside the conventional horizons of financial actors, and that without better data and disclosure, an orderly transition would become impossible.

Ten years on, the financial architecture Carney anticipated has been built. The sustainable debt market now sits at roughly $5 trillion in outstanding issuance.[2] Voluntary carbon credits, sustainability-linked bonds, transition finance, climate-risk-adjusted credit pricing — none existed at meaningful scale in 2015. All exist today. They were built on a single critical assumption: that the underlying emissions data is reliable.

It is not. The evidence — drawn from peer-reviewed atmospheric science, satellite measurement, central bank stress tests, and the first criminal prosecutions for fraud in voluntary carbon markets — converges on a single conclusion. The data primitive on which trillions of dollars of climate finance is priced is internally inconsistent, materially divergent from independent measurement, and structurally vulnerable to the same failure mode that brought down the subprime mortgage system in 2008.

This is the credibility gap. It is the largest unaddressed systemic risk in climate finance today, and the verification infrastructure needed to close it has not yet been built.

Subprime was a data story

It is worth recovering what the 2008 crisis was actually about. It is remembered as a housing crisis, but at its mechanical core, subprime was a data integrity failure with four layers.

The data primitive was a self-reported borrower attribute: income. So-called stated-income loans — sometimes called liar loans — let borrowers represent income without documentation.[3] The ratings layer above this primitive was supplied by Moody's, S&P, and Fitch, who assigned AAA grades to mortgage-backed securities built from these loans. The financial product layer turned the securities into collateralized debt obligations and synthetic CDOs, generating trillions of dollars in instruments at peak. The regulatory dependency layer was bank capital adequacy, where AAA-rated mortgage paper was treated as a safe asset against which institutions could hold minimal capital.

Each layer trusted the one below. The system worked until it didn't. When housing prices stalled and defaults rose, the ratings revealed themselves to be priced off data that had never been verified, and the entire stack repriced violently — not because housing fell, but because the data turned out to be wrong.

The lesson, often misstated, is not that financial innovation is dangerous. It is that any financial architecture stacked on an unverified data primitive will eventually fail to that primitive's failure mode.

The same architecture, now in climate

Climate finance has assembled the same four layers, with different content.

The data primitive is self-reported corporate emissions, organized under the Greenhouse Gas Protocol's Scope 1, 2, and 3 framework. Companies measure (or estimate, or extrapolate) their own emissions, file them through voluntary disclosure platforms like CDP, and submit them to standards bodies and ratings agencies.

The ratings layer is supplied by MSCI, Sustainalytics, S&P Global, Moody's ESG, CDP, and a long tail of specialized providers. They aggregate the self-reports, apply proprietary methodologies, and produce ESG and climate ratings that flow downstream into index construction, screening criteria, and benchmark portfolios.

The financial product layer is now substantial: the $5 trillion sustainable bond market, the rapidly growing sustainability-linked loan market, the voluntary carbon market (where credits are themselves derived from project-level self-reported claims), transition finance, and an emerging market for sustainability-linked derivatives.

The regulatory dependency layer is climate stress testing, financed-emissions reporting through frameworks like PCAF, and the supervisory architecture being developed by the Bank of England, the European Central Bank, the Reserve Bank of India, and the Network for Greening the Financial System.

Where the analogy stops: subprime had a sharp default trigger and a fixed time horizon. Climate's failure mode is slower — repricing happens through episodic disclosures, enforcement actions, and stress-test revelations rather than a single moment. But the asymmetric repricing risk — that financial assets priced on the assumption of accurate data will reprice when the data turns out to be wrong — is structurally identical.

Any financial architecture stacked on an unverified data primitive will eventually fail to that primitive's failure mode.

The data is wrong, and we can measure it

The empirical case that self-reported emissions diverge materially from physical reality is now too large to dismiss. Five strands of evidence, drawn from different methodologies and different jurisdictions, point the same direction.

Direct emissions: four times what was reported. In February 2025, the Environmental Defense Fund's MethaneSAT mission — purpose-built to measure methane emissions from oil and gas basins — published the first system-wide comparison of satellite-measured emissions against industry reports. United States oil and gas methane emissions were found to be more than four times higher than EPA estimates derived from industry self-reporting. Basins where natural gas accounts for at least 20% of production showed emissions three times higher than what was reported in official inventories.[4]

Cities: 18.3% under-reported on average. A 2021 Nature Communications study by Kevin Gurney and colleagues compared self-reported emission inventories from 48 US cities against the Vulcan dataset, which is calibrated to atmospheric measurements. The cities under-reported, on average, by 18.3%, with a range from -145.5% to +63.5%. If extrapolated to all US cities, the missing emissions exceed California's annual total by 23.5%.[5]

Corporate Scope 3: more than doubles when harmonized. A separate Nature Communications paper by Lena Klaaßen and Christian Stoll developed a harmonization framework for corporate carbon footprints. Across the studied sample, aggregate self-reported emissions of 360 MtCO₂e expanded to 751 MtCO₂e once omitted scope-3 categories were properly accounted for. The gap — 391 MtCO₂e — is roughly equivalent to Australia's annual greenhouse gas emissions.[6]

ESG ratings: less reliable than credit ratings by an order of magnitude. MIT Sloan's Aggregate Confusion Project — led by Florian Berg, Julian Kölbel, and Roberto Rigobon — measured the correlation among six major ESG raters at between 0.54 and 0.61. Credit ratings from Moody's and S&P are correlated at 0.92 to 0.99. The single largest driver of divergence is measurement: raters quantifying the same attribute and arriving at materially different values, accounting for 56% of the observed variance.[7]

Data providers: structurally inconsistent. A 2022 working paper from the European Commission's Joint Research Centre documented widespread inconsistencies between three commercial GHG data providers across every emissions category, every sector, and every year studied. The inconsistencies were large enough to flip carbon performance rankings between firms.[8]

What these studies have in common is that each compares self-reported data against an external reference: atmospheric measurement, satellite observation, harmonization methodology, or cross-provider triangulation. Wherever an external reference exists, the self-reported number is systematically off. Where no external reference exists, there is no reason to assume the data is more accurate.

The stack is $5 trillion and growing

The size of the financial architecture built on this data primitive is the reason a credibility gap becomes systemic.

The global sustainable bond market — green, social, sustainability, and sustainability-linked instruments — sits at approximately $5 trillion in outstanding issuance, according to the International Capital Market Association. ICMA describes it as the largest dedicated source of market finance for climate transition globally.[2]

Sustainability-linked bonds — debt whose coupon adjusts based on the issuer's emissions performance — issued $38 billion in 2024, a 41% drop from the prior year.[9] The contraction is itself diagnostic: market participants are pulling back precisely because they cannot verify the performance against which the instruments are priced. A market that cannot verify its underlying claim retracts toward instruments that can.

The voluntary carbon market — valued at roughly $2 billion before recent contractions — depends entirely on the credibility of self-reported, third-party-certified offset claims. A 2023 joint investigation by The Guardian, Die Zeit, and SourceMaterial, drawing on three peer-reviewed studies, found that 94% of credits issued under Verra's flagship rainforest protection program showed no climate benefit. Across the analyzed projects, the threat to forests had been overstated by approximately 400% on average. Verra had certified roughly three-quarters of all voluntary credits in the market.[10]

Scope 3 — the largest part of most corporate footprints and the area where verification is hardest — is structurally under-managed. The June 2024 Scope 3 Upstream report from Boston Consulting Group and CDP found that disclosed Scope 3 emissions are on average 26 times larger than direct (Scope 1 and 2) emissions. Yet only 15% of corporates disclosing to CDP have set a Scope 3 reduction target. The implied carbon liability across just three sectors — manufacturing, retail, and materials — exceeds $335 billion.[11]

The pattern is consistent. The financial stack assumes the data is good enough to price. The data is not.

The system is already cracking

The credibility gap is not a forecast. It is a chronicle in progress.

Central banks have stopped politely waiting. The European Central Bank's 2022 climate stress test of 104 European banks found that 60% lacked an integrated climate risk stress-testing framework. The supervisory chair at the time, Andrea Enria, called for urgent customer-level data engagement and identified data gaps as the central impediment. The ECB's own assessment described the exercise as exposing widespread deficiencies in data quality across institutions.[12]

Carbon markets have produced their first criminal fraud case. On October 2, 2024, the US Attorney for the Southern District of New York, the SEC, and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission unsealed coordinated criminal and civil charges against C-Quest Capital and its former CEO, Kenneth Newcombe — the first fraud actions in the voluntary carbon market. The allegations include falsifying data on cookstove deployment and emissions reductions to generate tens of millions of dollars in fraudulent credits sold into the market. Newcombe faces wire fraud, commodities fraud, and securities fraud charges. The company's former chief operating officer has pleaded guilty.[13]

Greenwashing enforcement has moved from warning to penalty. The SEC fined Invesco $17.5 million in November 2024 and WisdomTree $4 million in October 2024 for ESG misrepresentation. German prosecutors imposed a $27 million fine on Deutsche Bank's DWS in April 2025, following an earlier SEC fine, for overstating ESG integration in its funds.[14]

Corporate target accountability has functionally collapsed. A January 2025 Nature Climate Change study examined 1,041 corporate emissions targets that ended in 2020. Of those, 88 firms (9%) publicly missed their targets and 320 firms (31%) simply stopped reporting on them. Only three of the 88 failed firms received media coverage.[15] When the data primitive cannot be verified and accountability for missed targets is essentially absent, the gap between disclosed climate commitments and actual emissions performance is unconstrained.

The credit market did not solve its data problem by asking borrowers to fill out longer forms. Climate finance needs the equivalent architectural answer.

The infrastructure climate finance is missing

The fix is not more disclosure. The credit market did not solve its data quality problem in the 1980s by asking borrowers to fill out longer forms. It built credit bureaus, FICO scores, securitization standards, and the data infrastructure that priced creditworthiness independently of the borrower's word. The financial system trusted the data because something other than the borrower had verified it.

Climate finance needs the equivalent architectural layer. Three properties define what credible verification must look like.

Asset-level granularity. Emissions are physical. They occur at specific facilities, in specific quantities, at specific times. Aggregating to the company level — the unit at which most disclosure occurs — destroys the signal that financial markets need to price climate risk. A bank's exposure to a steel sector transition is not its exposure to "steel companies"; it is its exposure to specific blast furnaces, with specific emissions profiles and specific decarbonization economics.

Independence from the reporter. The reporter is the conflicted party. Verification must come from sources that do not depend on the company's cooperation: satellite observation, atmospheric measurement, sensor networks, and supply chain primary data validated through trustless cryptographic mechanisms. Self-reporting can be one input. It cannot be the foundation.

Trustless data exchange. Verification at scale requires firms to share operational data — emissions, energy consumption, fuel mix — with counterparties who may also be competitors. A federated architecture, where primary data is hashed and encrypted at source and only verified outputs are shared, resolves the privacy-versus-verification tradeoff that has stalled supply-chain transparency for two decades.

The convergence happening in 2024 through 2026 — open satellite data programs from Sentinel, Landsat, MODIS, and TROPOMI; commercial-grade geospatial AI; and federated data architectures — has made this verification layer technically feasible for the first time. The cost of monitoring 900,000 industrial assets from orbit is now an order of magnitude lower than it was five years ago.

The question for climate finance leadership is no longer whether the verification layer will be built. It is whether it will be built proactively — by the actors who depend on it: banks, regulators, asset managers, corporate buyers of carbon credits — or whether it will be built reactively, after a market dislocation forces the issue.

In 2008, the verification layer was built after the crisis. The chronicle of the next decade in climate finance will be written by whoever decides to build it first.

About the research

This analysis synthesizes peer-reviewed evidence on emissions data quality, central bank climate supervisory reports, regulatory enforcement actions, and published market statistics from the period 2021 through early 2026. Where multiple sources reported a single underlying study, primary references are cited. All currency figures are reported in US dollars unless otherwise specified. Market sizing for sustainable finance reflects ICMA's published estimates as of mid-2024.

DEFINE Research maintains independence from the platforms, raters, and issuers discussed. Corrections, additional source leads, and methodological feedback are welcomed at research@define.global.

D

DEFINE Research

DEFINE is an AI-powered climate intelligence platform that monitors physical assets worldwide and converts satellite signals into decision-grade carbon, climate risk, and supply chain data — independent of corporate self-reporting. DEFINE Research is the platform's editorial arm, producing analysis on climate finance, regulation, and verification infrastructure for policymakers, investors, and industry leaders.
